iti49:1.1: This was said by the Blessed One, said by the Arahant, so I have heard:
iti49:2.1:
“Overcome by two viewpoints, monks, some human & divine beings adhere,
other human & divine beings slip right past, while those with vision see.
iti49:3.2:
Human & divine beings enjoy becoming, delight in becoming, are satisfied with becoming.
When the Dhamma is being taught for the sake of the cessation of becoming,
their minds do not take to it, are not calmed by it, do not settle on it or become resolved on it.
iti49:4.1: “And how do some slip right past?
iti49:4.2: Some, feeling horrified, humiliated, & disgusted with that very becoming, relish non-becoming:
iti49:4.3: ‘When this self, at the break-up of the body, after death, perishes & is destroyed,
iti49:4.4: and does not exist after death, that is peaceful, that is exquisite, that is sufficiency!’
iti49:4.5: This is how some slip right past.
iti49:5.1: “And how do those with vision see?
iti49:5.2: There is the case where a monk sees what’s come to be as what’s come to be.
iti49:5.3:
Seeing what’s come to be as what’s come to be, he practices for disenchantment with what’s come to be,
dispassion toward what’s come to be, cessation of what’s come to be.
iti49:5.4: This is how those with vision see.”
iti49:6.1: Those, having seen what’s come to be as what’s come to be,
iti49:6.2: and what’s gone beyond what’s come to be,
iti49:6.3: are released in line with what’s come to be,
iti49:6.4: through the exhaustion of craving for becoming.
iti49:7.1: If they’ve comprehended what’s come to be,
iti49:7.2: and are free from the craving for becoming & non-,
iti49:7.3: with the non-becoming of what’s come to be,
iti49:7.4: monks come to no further becoming.
